1: The command to return a neighbor's straying ox or sheep — not to ignore it — encodes the neighbor-love ethic in a concrete action. Jesus's parable of the Good Samaritan (Luke 10:30–37) works out who counts as "neighbor" and what love requires of them. The lost sheep parable (Luke 15:4–7) mirrors this command: the shepherd who leaves the ninety-nine to find the one straying is the type of Christ who seeks the lost. He is the shepherd who never ignores a wandering sheep (John 10:11–16).
2: If the owner cannot be found, care for the animal until the owner comes. The principle of faithful stewardship of what belongs to another — held in trust until the owner's return. Christ's parables of the talents (Matthew 25:14–30) and the minas (Luke 19:12–27) build on this stewardship structure: the servants hold the master's goods in trust until he returns and requires an accounting. Faithful holding of what is entrusted is the model of the Christian life until Christ's return.
3: "Do the same with his donkey, his garment, or any lost property." The general principle behind the specific examples: active restoration of what belongs to a neighbor, not passive disregard. Jesus applies the same principle to persons who are lost: the lost coin and lost son parables (Luke 15:8–32) show the same "do not leave it, restore it" pattern applied to human lives. The lost-and-found dynamic is the gospel narrative in miniature.
4: Help your neighbor lift his fallen ox or donkey — do not ignore it. The Sabbath controversy (Luke 13:10–17; Deuteronomy 14:5) involves Jesus citing this very principle: "which of you, if his son or his ox falls into a well, will not immediately pull it out on the Sabbath?" The command to help a fallen animal becomes one of his arguments for releasing the woman bent double — if you help your fallen ox, how much more the daughter of Abraham bound by Satan?
5: A woman must not wear men's clothing, nor a man women's clothing — both are an abomination to YHWH. The command upholds distinctions established in the created order. Christ as the one through whom all things were created (John 1:3; Colossians 1:16) is the ground of the created order that these distinctions protect. The new creation Christ inaugurates does not abolish the distinctions of creation but redeems them (Galatians 3:28 — "neither male nor female" refers to equal standing before God, not to the erasure of created distinctions).
6: Do not take the mother bird with the young — let the mother go. The law protects natural reproductive capacity and the continuity of life. Jesus's observation that "not one sparrow falls to the ground apart from your Father" (Matthew 10:29) reflects the same divine attentiveness to the welfare of birds. The Creator who commanded this law is the Son through whom "all things hold together" (Colossians 1:17) — the care embedded in the Torah reflects the care of the one who made and sustains all creatures.
7: "Let the mother fly away and take only the young. Then things will go well for you, and you will have long life." The life-promise attached to the bird law is structurally similar to the life-promise attached to honoring parents (Deuteronomy 5:16). Paul cites the honoring-parents command as the first commandment with a promise (Ephesians 6:2). The life-promise pattern throughout the law points to Christ who is himself "the life" (John 14:6) and who gives eternal life to those in him.
8: "Install a railing around the roof so that you do not incur bloodguilt if someone falls." The law of protective construction — positive duty to prevent foreseeable harm to others. Christ's concern extends to the protection of "the least of these" (Matthew 25:40–45); the failure to prevent foreseeable harm is culpable in the new covenant as in the old. The whole trajectory of the law toward the protection of life is fulfilled in Christ who is "the Prince of Life" (Acts 3:15) and who lays down his own life to protect others (John 10:11).
9: "Do not plant two kinds of seed in your vineyard." The mixture-prohibition keeps categories distinct. Paul applies the yoke-image spiritually in 2 Corinthians 6:14 ("do not be unequally yoked with unbelievers"), carrying the principle of non-mixing into the new covenant community. Christ's community is defined by a single allegiance (Matthew 6:24: "no one can serve two masters") — the mixture-prohibition becomes the call to undivided loyalty.
10: "Do not plow with an ox and a donkey yoked together." The unequal yoke violates the created order of kinds and is unfair to both animals. Paul cites the principle in 2 Corinthians 6:14. Christ's own yoke is described as "easy" and "light" (Matthew 11:29–30) — not because the demands of discipleship are low, but because the one who carries it with you is the Son of God. His yoke fits; mixed yokes grind.
11: "Do not wear clothing woven from a mixture of wool and linen." The sha'atnez law, like the seed and yoke prohibitions, maintains distinctions. In Hebrews 10:20 the veil of the temple, representing Christ's flesh, is "the new and living way he opened for us." The concern for what is pure and unmixed in covenant worship culminates in Christ, the one truly pure mediator, without the mixture of sin that mars all other human priests (Hebrews 7:26).
12: "Make tassels on the four corners of your outer garment." The tsitsit (tassels) were worn to remember all YHWH's commandments (Numbers 15:38–40). The woman with the twelve-year hemorrhage touched "the fringe of his garment" (Matthew 9:20 — Greek kraspedon, the same word as LXX for tsitsit). Crowds in Gennesaret brought the sick to touch the fringe of Jesus's cloak (Matthew 14:36; Mark 6:56). The tassel that symbolized Torah-obedience, when touched on Jesus's garment, brought healing — he is the living Torah, and touching him restores what the law could only describe.
13: The case of the man who falsely slanders his bride's virginity — the law protects women against malicious defamation within the covenant's most intimate institution. Christ's protection of the vulnerable and his condemnation of hypocritical public accusation are expressed throughout the Gospels. His rebuke of those who publicly accused the woman caught in adultery (John 8:3–11) operates within the same concern: the covenant community's sexual ethics must be maintained without becoming an instrument of predation.
14: Public slander against a bride's honor was both a personal attack and an attack on the family. The judicial process — evidence brought to the elders at the gate — represents the covenant's commitment to truth over accusation. Christ is the one who silences false accusers (Revelation 12:10: "the accuser of our brothers has been thrown down"). He is the defender of those unjustly accused, the advocate before the Father (1 John 2:1).
15: The parents bring the evidence — the truth matters, and there is recourse for the falsely accused. The covenant system provides a mechanism for vindicating the truth. Christ's own resurrection is the ultimate vindication of one who was falsely accused: the trial that condemned him as a blasphemer (Matthew 26:65) is overturned by the Father who raised him from the dead (Acts 2:24; Romans 1:4). The resurrection is YHWH's verdict in the case.
16: The father's testimony at the gate on his daughter's behalf — a public defense of a family member. The Father's witness to the Son at his baptism ("this is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased," Matthew 3:17) and at the transfiguration (Matthew 17:5) is the divine Father standing as witness and advocate for the Son. The covenant pattern of family defense and vindication reaches its apex in the Father's testimony to and for Christ.
17: The evidence of virginity spread before the elders — physical evidence required for conviction or acquittal. The covenant's commitment to evidentiary truth-seeking is fulfilled in Christ's resurrection, which Paul describes as verified by over five hundred witnesses (1 Corinthians 15:5–8): the risen Christ appeared publicly, providing the evidence that cannot be denied. The gospel is not private speculation but publicly evidenced historical event.
18: The slanderous husband is punished and fined — false accusation has judicial consequences. Those who falsely accused Christ faced the same principle: Peter charges the Jerusalem crowd with responsibility for condemning "the Holy and Righteous One" (Acts 3:14). The covenant's accountability for false accusation becomes the ground of the apostolic call to repentance — "you denied the Holy One... but God raised him from the dead" (Acts 3:14–15).
19: The fine goes to the father; the woman remains the wife permanently — the slander attempted to dissolve the marriage, and the law makes it permanent instead. The slanderous attempt backfires. Christ's opponents sought to destroy him, but their act of destruction became the means of the new covenant's permanent establishment. The cross that was meant to end his influence makes his relationship with his people indissoluble (Romans 8:38–39: nothing can separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus).
20: If the charge of non-virginity is true, the judgment follows. The law holds both directions: false accusation is punished, but actual covenant violation is also judged. The gospel holds the same both directions: "there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1) applies to those who are truly in him; those who continue in sin are not protected by a claim to covenant membership (1 John 1:6; Deuteronomy 3:6).
21: "Purge the evil from your midst" — the recurring formula that closes capital cases in Deuteronomy. The community's integrity requires the removal of that which corrupts. Paul cites this Deuteronomic "purge" principle when addressing the Corinthian sexual scandal: "purge the evil person from among you" (1 Corinthians 5:13, quoting Deuteronomy 17:7; Deuteronomy 22:21). The community of Christ, like Israel, is called to be a holy community; tolerating public unrepentant sin within it undermines the witness of the covenant people.
22: "If a man is caught having sex with another man's wife, both shall die." The equal accountability of both parties in adultery — a protection against the double standard that typically penalized only the woman. Jesus's response to the woman caught in adultery (John 8:3–11) operates within this framework: the accusers brought only the woman, violating the law's own requirement that both parties be judged. His challenge — "let him who is without sin cast the first stone" — does not abolish the law but exposes the accusers' own guilt.
23: The law distinguishes between the betrothed woman in the city and in the country — coercion is assessed contextually. The principle of contextual justice — reading the circumstances to determine culpability — reflects the covenant's concern for truth rather than merely formal compliance. Christ's judicial wisdom operates the same way: "do not judge by appearances, but judge with right judgment" (John 7:24). His verdicts attend to the actual situation, not merely the outward category.
24: "Stone them to death" for the city case — the severity reflects both the sexual ethic and the assumption that a cry for help would have been heard in the city. The city as the place of public accountability is the background for Jesus's weeping over Jerusalem (Matthew 23:37–38) — the city that kills the prophets and those sent to it. Jerusalem's failure to "cry out" in response to Christ's coming brings judgment (Luke 19:41–44).
25: In the country rape case, only the man dies — the woman's coercion is presumed and she bears no guilt. The principle of non-imputation of guilt for the coerced — those who sin against their will — is the negative image of Paul's substitution theology: Christ bore the guilt of those who sinned willingly (Romans 5:12–19). The law's protection of the coerced victim is a shadow of the gospel's liberation of those held captive by sin they did not choose.
26: "Do nothing to the young woman — she has done nothing deserving death. This case is like that of a man who attacks and kills his neighbor." The law explicitly extends the murder analogy to sexual assault. The seriousness with which the law treats rape as equivalent to murder underscores the dignity of persons. Christ's consistent attention to those who have been violated and excluded — the woman with the flow of blood, the Samaritan woman, the woman caught in adultery — enacts this dignity-protection in person.
27: "Even if the betrothed woman cried out, there was no one to rescue her." The law imagines the victim crying out with no one to hear — the scenario of the abandoned. Lamentations 1:2 uses this image for Jerusalem: "she cried out, but there was no one to comfort her." Christ enters precisely this situation — his cry from the cross ("my God, my God, why have you forsaken me," Matthew 27:46, citing Psalms 22:1) is the cry of the abandoned, taken on in solidarity with all who cry out and find no rescuer.
28: The unbetrothed virgin who is violated — the man must pay the bride-price and marry her, and may never divorce her. The principle of permanent responsibility arising from a wrongful act: the one who violated must provide. This anticipates Christ's permanent commitment to those he has redeemed: "I give them eternal life, and they will never perish" (John 10:28). The covenant bond is irrevocable, secured not by the violated party's merit but by the one who initiates the relationship.
29: Fifty shekels of silver — the standard bride-price — paid to the father. The economic consequences of violation are real. Christ "redeemed" his people at the price of his own blood (1 Peter 1:18–19: "not with perishable things such as silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ"). Where the law measured violation in silver shekels, the new covenant's redemption is priced in the blood of the Son of God — an infinitely greater payment for an infinitely greater transgression.
30: "A man must not marry his father's wife or violate his father's marital rights." The law prohibits incest and the appropriation of a father's household through marriage to his wife. Paul cites the principle of Deuteronomy 22:30 when addressing the Corinthian man living with his father's wife (1 Corinthians 5:1): "immorality of a kind not even found among Gentiles." The covenant community's purity standards were meant to exceed surrounding cultures, as a witness to the character of YHWH — and of Christ who is "holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners" (Hebrews 7:26).
